title: Best Employee Scheduling Software 2026
description: Stop building schedules in spreadsheets. The best employee scheduling software in 2026 for hourly teams, shift workers, and service businesses.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Why Scheduling Software Pays for Itself Fast

The average manager spends 3-5 hours per week building and adjusting schedules. Multiply that by 50 weeks and it's 150-250 hours per year — the equivalent of 3-6 weeks of a manager's time dedicated entirely to admin. Dedicated scheduling software cuts that time by 70-80% and reduces the costly errors that come from spreadsheet-based scheduling: understaffing during peak hours, overstaffing during slow periods, missed shift swaps, and compliance violations.

We evaluated five scheduling tools specifically for the types of businesses that need them most: restaurants, retail, healthcare, hospitality, and service businesses with hourly workers.

1. Deputy — Best All-Around Scheduling Software

Price: From $3.50/user/month (Scheduling only) / $4.90/user/month (Premium)

Deputy handles scheduling, time tracking, leave management, and payroll integrations in one platform. The AI-powered scheduling feature analyzes historical demand data and suggests optimal shift coverage — useful for businesses with variable demand like restaurants and retail stores.

The mobile app is strong: employees can clock in/out, swap shifts, view their schedule, and request time off from their phones. Push notifications for schedule changes eliminate the "I didn't know about the shift" excuse. Deputy integrates with over 300 payroll and POS systems, which reduces data re-entry between systems.

Best for: Multi-location businesses, restaurants, and retail operations that need demand-based scheduling and strong payroll integration.

2. When I Work — Best for Simple Shift Scheduling

Price: Free (up to 75 users) / From $2.50/user/month

When I Work prioritizes simplicity. The scheduling interface is clean: drag shifts onto a calendar grid, assign them to employees, publish, and notify. Employees get text and push notifications. Shift swapping and availability requests happen through the app with manager approval.

The free plan covering 75 users is exceptional value — most small businesses with hourly workforces won't need to upgrade for basic scheduling. The paid tier adds time tracking, attendance reports, and more advanced scheduling features.

Best for: Small businesses with straightforward shift scheduling needs that want a tool their team will actually use without training.

3. Homebase — Best for Small Hourly Teams

Price: Free (1 location) / From $24.95/month/location

Homebase targets small businesses with hourly workforces: local restaurants, coffee shops, salons, and retail stores. The free plan is legitimately useful: unlimited employees at one location, basic scheduling, time clock, and messaging. No user caps, no expiring trial.

Homebase goes beyond scheduling with HR tools: onboarding paperwork, PTO tracking, compliance alerts for labor law violations (like scheduling someone below minimum rest periods), and hiring tools. For a small business owner wearing many hats, having scheduling and basic HR in one tool reduces operational complexity.

Best for: Local small businesses with a single location and hourly workforces who want scheduling bundled with basic HR tools.

4. Connecteam — Best for Deskless Worker Communication

Price: Free (up to 10 users) / From $29/month (up to 30 users)

Connecteam is built for the deskless workforce: field service teams, construction crews, cleaning companies, and delivery operations. Beyond scheduling and time tracking, it includes internal communication (chat, announcements), task management, training, and digital forms — all in a mobile-first app.

For businesses where workers are never at a desk and need to stay coordinated through their phones, Connecteam provides more communication tools than any other scheduling platform. The free plan covers up to 10 users with full features, which is a complete solution for very small operations.

Best for: Field service businesses, construction, cleaning companies, and any operation where workers are mobile and need communication tools alongside scheduling.

5. 7shifts — Best for Restaurants Specifically

Price: Free (up to 30 employees, 1 location) / From $29.99/month/location

7shifts is purpose-built for the restaurant industry. The scheduling interface is designed around the specific way restaurants organize shifts: FOH, BOH, separate role templates, labor cost percentage targets, and integration with major POS systems (Toast, Square, Clover, Lightspeed).

The labor cost forecasting feature is particularly valuable: as you build the schedule, 7shifts shows your projected labor cost percentage against your target in real time. For restaurant operators watching food and labor costs as primary business metrics, this keeps scheduling decisions grounded in profitability.

The free plan covering 30 employees at one location covers most independent restaurants completely.

Best for: Restaurant operators who want scheduling software that understands their specific operational structure and POS integrations.

Quick Comparison

ToolFree PlanPaid FromBest For
DeputyNo$3.50/user/moMulti-location businesses
When I WorkYes (75 users)$2.50/user/moSimple scheduling
HomebaseYes (1 location)$24.95/mo/locationLocal small businesses
ConnecteamYes (10 users)$29/mo (30 users)Field/deskless workers
7shiftsYes (30 employees)$29.99/mo/locationRestaurants

What to Consider Before Choosing

The right scheduling tool depends on your specific context:

  • Industry: Restaurants should start with 7shifts. Field service teams should look at Connecteam first. General retail and hospitality fit When I Work or Homebase.
  • Number of locations: Single location businesses have the most free options. Multi-location businesses should look at Deputy or When I Work's per-user pricing, which scales better than per-location pricing.
  • Payroll integration: If you need scheduling to feed directly into payroll, Deputy has the broadest integration library. Check your payroll provider's app marketplace before committing to a scheduling tool.
  • Mobile adoption: All five tools have mobile apps, but ease of use varies. When I Work and 7shifts consistently score highest for employee adoption in reviews.

The Hidden Cost of Bad Scheduling

Beyond manager time, poor scheduling creates downstream costs: overtime that could have been avoided, last-minute staffing agency fees when someone no-shows, and turnover from employees who feel their time isn't respected. The ROI on scheduling software isn't just about time savings — it's about running a tighter operation with fewer expensive surprises.

Building the Full Ops Stack

Scheduling is one piece of running an efficient operation. For customer management, see our guide to the best free CRM tools. For communication between your team, our team communication tools guide covers the leading options.

  • Best Team Communication Tools in 2026
  • Best Helpdesk Software for Small Business in 2026
  • Best Free CRM Tools in 2026

title: Best Webflow Alternatives in 2026
description: Webflow is powerful but expensive and complex. We tested the top Webflow alternatives including Framer, Squarespace, Wix Studio, WordPress, and Cargo to find the best fit for different budgets and skill levels.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Why Look Beyond Webflow?

Webflow earns its reputation. The visual development environment is genuinely impressive, giving designers near-total control over HTML and CSS without writing code directly. But in 2026, it comes with real drawbacks that push a lot of users toward alternatives.

First, the price. Webflow's CMS plan runs $23/month (billed annually) and the Business plan hits $39/month before you've added any e-commerce. Second, the learning curve is steep — Webflow is closer to coding than most website builders, and users who aren't comfortable with CSS concepts like flexbox and grid will struggle. Third, if you're a freelancer or agency building sites for clients, Webflow's client billing model adds friction and cost.

The good news is that several strong alternatives have matured significantly in 2026. Here's what we found after building real projects on each platform.

1. Framer — Best for Product and SaaS Landing Pages

Framer has evolved from a prototyping tool into a legitimate website builder, and it's become the favorite platform for SaaS companies and product designers who want polished, high-performance sites without Webflow's complexity.

What Makes Framer Stand Out

  • Speed: Framer sites consistently score 95+ on Google PageSpeed Insights. The static site output is among the fastest we've tested.
  • Design quality: The component system and interactions are excellent. Framer sites look professional out of the box, and the built-in animation tools are more intuitive than Webflow's interaction designer.
  • CMS: Framer's CMS handles blog posts, case studies, and dynamic content cleanly. It's not as powerful as Webflow's CMS but covers 90% of use cases.
  • AI tools: Framer's AI copywriting and layout generation have improved noticeably. You can generate an entire landing page from a prompt and customize from there.

Framer Limitations

  • E-commerce is not built in — you'll need to integrate Stripe or an external store
  • The free plan limits you to one published site with Framer branding
  • Complex multi-page sites can get unwieldy without careful component organization
  • Pricing: Free plan available; Pro is $10/month, Site plans start at $19/month

Best For

Startups, SaaS companies, and designers who want a fast, beautiful landing page or marketing site. Framer hits a sweet spot between design control and ease of use that Webflow misses for many users.

2. Squarespace — Best for Service and Creative Businesses

Squarespace continues to be the gold standard for visually polished websites aimed at service businesses, creatives, and local companies. If your business lives or dies on how it looks — photography, interior design, restaurants, boutique retail — Squarespace templates are hard to beat.

What Makes Squarespace Stand Out

  • Template quality: Every Squarespace template is professionally designed and genuinely beautiful. You start looking good before you've customized anything.
  • Simplicity: The editor is more opinionated than Webflow, which means less flexibility but a much shorter learning curve. Most users have a site live in under two hours.
  • Built-in features: Squarespace includes blogging, scheduling (Acuity integration), member areas, e-commerce, and email marketing in one platform.
  • SEO: Clean URL structures, auto-generated sitemaps, and solid meta tag controls make Squarespace a reasonable choice for SEO-conscious businesses.

Squarespace Limitations

  • Less design flexibility than Webflow or Framer — you work within template constraints
  • No app marketplace comparable to Wix or WordPress
  • E-commerce transaction fees on the Basic Commerce plan (0% only on Advanced at $65/month)
  • Pricing: Personal $16/month, Business $23/month, Commerce from $28/month

Best For

Creatives, photographers, restaurants, local service businesses, and anyone who wants a beautiful site without a learning curve.

3. Wix Studio — Best for Freelancers and Agencies

Wix Studio is Wix's answer to Webflow — a professional-grade design environment aimed at freelancers and agencies building client sites. Launched in 2023 and refined significantly since, Wix Studio in 2026 is a genuinely capable alternative for agency work.

What Makes Wix Studio Stand Out

  • Client management: Wix Studio has the best client collaboration tools on this list. You can invite clients to review, approve, and edit their own site with role-based permissions.
  • Design freedom: Unlike standard Wix, Wix Studio uses a grid-based layout system that gives you precise control over positioning without the CSS knowledge Webflow requires.
  • Responsive design: Wix Studio's breakpoint editor is excellent — you set layouts for desktop, tablet, and mobile independently with visual controls.
  • Business tools: Booking, payments, CRM, and marketing tools are all built in, which matters for client sites that need these features.

Wix Studio Limitations

  • Still newer than Webflow — some advanced features feel less polished
  • Performance can lag Framer and Squarespace on PageSpeed scores
  • The ecosystem of Wix Studio-specific resources (templates, components) is smaller than standard Wix

Best For

Freelancers and small agencies who build client websites and need strong collaboration features, flexible design control, and built-in business tools without requiring CSS expertise.

4. WordPress — Best for Long-Term Scalability

WordPress powering 43% of the web is not an accident. If you're building something that might grow significantly — a content site, a membership platform, a complex e-commerce store, an LMS — WordPress offers unlimited flexibility that no hosted builder can match.

What Makes WordPress Stand Out

  • Ecosystem: 60,000+ plugins cover virtually any functionality you can imagine. If you need it, there's likely a plugin for it.
  • Block editor (Gutenberg): The modern WordPress editor with full-site editing (FSE) rivals many website builders for visual design control. Page builders like Elementor and Bricks push this further.
  • Cost: WordPress itself is free. Pair it with quality hosting (starting around $4-10/month) and you have a full website platform for minimal ongoing cost.
  • Ownership: Your data, your site, your code. No platform risk of price increases or feature removal.

WordPress Limitations

  • Requires maintenance (updates, security, backups) that hosted builders handle automatically
  • Setup takes significantly longer than any hosted builder
  • Quality varies enormously by theme and plugins chosen
  • Security management is your responsibility

Best For

Businesses planning significant content production, developers who want full control, and anyone who needs functionality beyond what hosted builders offer. If you want to learn more about building with WordPress, see our guide to the best website builders for small business.

5. Cargo — Best for Portfolios and Artists

Cargo occupies a unique niche: it's built specifically for creative portfolios and artist websites, with an aesthetic and feature set designed around visual work rather than business conversion. If you're a designer, illustrator, photographer, or artist, Cargo deserves serious consideration.

What Makes Cargo Stand Out

  • Design identity: Cargo's templates have a distinctive, editorial quality that sets portfolio sites apart from generic templates on Squarespace or Wix.
  • Full-bleed layouts: Image-first layouts that make visual work look exceptional
  • Simplicity for creatives: The editing experience is stripped down and focused — no overwhelming dashboards or feature bloat
  • Community: Cargo has a genuine community of designers and artists. Being on Cargo carries a certain creative credibility.

Cargo Limitations

  • Not a general-purpose website builder — poor choice for business or e-commerce
  • Limited SEO capabilities compared to other platforms
  • Smaller template library than Squarespace or Wix
  • Pricing: Free plan available; Pro is $13/month

Best For

Visual artists, photographers, illustrators, and designers who want a portfolio that looks genuinely different from mainstream templates.

How These Alternatives Compare to Webflow

  • Design control: Webflow > Framer > Wix Studio > Squarespace > WordPress (with page builder) > Cargo
  • Ease of use: Squarespace > Cargo > Wix Studio > Framer > WordPress > Webflow
  • Performance: Framer > Squarespace > Wix Studio ≈ Webflow > WordPress (varies) > Cargo
  • Price (entry paid): Cargo ($13) > Framer ($10) > WordPress ($4-10 hosting) > Squarespace ($16) > Wix Studio ($17) > Webflow ($23)
  • E-commerce: WordPress > Squarespace > Wix Studio > Webflow > Framer (via integration) > Cargo (minimal)

Which Webflow Alternative Should You Choose?

The right choice depends entirely on your use case. For SaaS and product marketing sites, Framer delivers the best performance-to-ease ratio. For service businesses and creatives, Squarespace offers the most polished out-of-box templates. For agency and freelance work, Wix Studio has the best client collaboration features. For maximum long-term flexibility, WordPress remains unmatched. And for pure creative portfolios, Cargo stands apart.

None of these alternatives is universally better than Webflow — they're different tradeoffs. Webflow's combination of design control and CMS power is still unique. But for most use cases, one of these alternatives will serve you better at lower cost and complexity.

If you're also looking for tools to manage content, email, and funnels alongside your website, Systeme.io bundles website building with email marketing, funnels, and course creation in one affordable platform — worth considering if you're building an online business rather than just a marketing site.

Related Reading

  • Best Website Builder for Small Business in 2026
  • Best Landing Page Builders in 2026
  • Best SEO Tools in 2026: Tested and Ranked

title: Best Screen Recording Software in 2026
description: We recorded, edited, and shared hundreds of videos across Loom, OBS, ScreenPal, Camtasia, and Tella to find the best screen recording software for every use case in 2026.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Screen Recording Has Become a Core Business Skill

In 2026, the ability to record your screen quickly and share useful video has moved from a nice-to-have to an essential workflow for remote teams, customer support, product teams, and content creators. Whether you're recording a product demo, explaining a bug to a developer, creating onboarding videos, or building a YouTube tutorial, the right screen recording software makes the difference between a polished, professional output and a frustrating, time-consuming process.

We spent three weeks creating real projects with each of the major screen recording tools — walkthroughs, tutorials, async team updates, and customer-facing demos. Here's what we found.

Quick Comparison: Who Each Tool Is For

  • Loom: Best for async team communication and quick customer demos
  • OBS Studio: Best for live streaming and advanced recording setups
  • ScreenPal: Best budget option for educators and trainers
  • Camtasia: Best for polished training videos and course content
  • Tella: Best for beautiful, camera-forward content creators

1. Loom — Best for Business and Team Communication

Loom has become the default async video tool for business teams, and in 2026 it remains the most frictionless option for recording and sharing videos internally or with customers. You install the Chrome extension or desktop app, click record, and get a shareable link in seconds after you stop.

What Loom Does Best

  • Speed to share: Video is uploaded and shareable almost instantly after recording. No waiting for rendering or manual upload steps.
  • Viewer experience: Loom's video player includes a table of contents (auto-generated from chapters you set), emoji reactions, comments at specific timestamps, and a transcript. Viewers engage with Loom videos, not just watch them.
  • AI features: Loom's AI now auto-generates summaries, titles, and action items from your recording. It also removes filler words and awkward pauses automatically — a huge time saver.
  • Team features: Shared libraries, workspaces, and view analytics (who watched, how long) make Loom genuinely useful for managing async communication.

Loom Limitations

  • The free plan is now limited to 25 videos (previously unlimited) — a significant downgrade
  • No advanced video editing — cuts and annotations only, no full timeline editor
  • Not suitable for live streaming
  • Pricing: Starter $15/user/month, Business $25/user/month

Best For

Sales teams sending personalized demos, product teams recording feedback, customer success sharing walkthroughs, and remote teams replacing unnecessary meetings with async video updates.

2. OBS Studio — Best for Advanced Recording and Live Streaming

OBS (Open Broadcaster Software) is free, open-source, and the tool of choice for anyone who needs serious control over their recording or streaming setup. It's not a casual tool — OBS has a learning curve — but what you get in return is unmatched flexibility.

What OBS Does Best

  • Free: Completely free with no feature limits, watermarks, or recording length restrictions
  • Scene switching: Set up multiple scenes (different camera angles, screen layouts, overlay combinations) and switch between them live or on a schedule
  • Multi-source recording: Combine multiple monitors, multiple cameras, game capture, browser sources, and audio inputs simultaneously
  • Streaming: Built-in streaming to Twitch, YouTube, Facebook Live, and custom RTMP endpoints
  • Plugins: A massive plugin ecosystem extends OBS with features like replay buffers, virtual cameras, and advanced audio processing

OBS Limitations

  • Steep learning curve — expect 2-4 hours to get comfortable with the interface
  • No built-in sharing or cloud storage — you record locally and distribute files yourself
  • No video editing — OBS records; you need a separate tool to edit
  • Not designed for quick async sharing like Loom

Best For

Streamers, podcasters, content creators who need multi-source setups, educators recording long-form tutorials, and anyone who needs professional recording without paying for software.

3. ScreenPal — Best Budget Option for Educators

ScreenPal (formerly Screencast-O-Matic) targets teachers, trainers, and small businesses that need solid screen recording with built-in editing at an affordable price. The free plan is genuinely functional, and the paid plans are among the most affordable in the category.

What ScreenPal Does Best

  • Price: Free plan records up to 15 minutes; Solo Deluxe is $4/month — the most affordable paid screen recorder we tested
  • Built-in editor: ScreenPal includes a timeline-based video editor with cuts, captions (auto-generated), transitions, and overlays without needing a separate app
  • Hosting: ScreenPal includes cloud hosting and a shareable link with every plan, including free
  • Ease of use: Setup takes minutes. The interface is simple enough for non-technical users.
  • Captions: Auto-generated captions are surprisingly accurate, which matters for accessibility and engagement

ScreenPal Limitations

  • Free plan adds a watermark
  • Less polished viewer experience than Loom
  • No AI-powered features like Loom's auto-summaries
  • Team collaboration features are basic compared to enterprise tools

Best For

Educators, trainers, small business owners, and individuals who need recording + basic editing + hosting without paying $15+/month for Loom or $300+ for Camtasia.

4. Camtasia — Best for Professional Training Video Production

Camtasia from TechSmith is the veteran of the category, and it remains the gold standard for producing polished, professional training videos, software demos, and eLearning content. If your output needs to look truly finished — with animations, callouts, zooms, and multi-track editing — Camtasia is in a category of its own.

What Camtasia Does Best

  • Full video editor: Camtasia is a proper multi-track video editor built specifically for screen recordings. Cuts, zooms, annotations, callouts, transitions, and animations are all built in.
  • Smart features: Cursor effects (highlight, magnify, spotlight), screen zoom-and-pan animations, and click animations make technical content dramatically clearer
  • Assets library: Camtasia includes a library of professional motion graphics, music, and intro/outro templates
  • Quizzes: For eLearning, Camtasia can embed interactive quizzes directly in exported videos (SCORM compatible)
  • PowerPoint integration: Record directly from within PowerPoint and add recording tracks alongside slide content

Camtasia Limitations

  • Price: Camtasia costs $179.88/year or $299.99 one-time license — significantly more expensive than alternatives
  • Windows and Mac only — no browser-based recording
  • Export and rendering is slower than cloud-based tools
  • Not designed for quick sharing — it's a production tool, not an async communication tool

Best For

Corporate training teams, instructional designers, course creators, and software companies that produce polished tutorial content as a core part of their product or service.

5. Tella — Best for Content Creators Who Lead with Camera

Tella is the newest tool on this list and the most design-forward. Where Loom treats the camera as secondary to the screen recording, Tella is built for creators who want their face and personality front and center. The result is a screen recorder that produces content that feels more like a YouTube video or newsletter episode than a screen capture.

What Tella Does Best

  • Visual design: Tella videos look genuinely beautiful. Camera layouts, backgrounds, text overlays, and transitions are polished in a way that makes your content look intentional and professional
  • Layouts: Switch between full-screen camera, picture-in-picture, side-by-side, and screenshare-only within a single recording
  • Chapters and scenes: Record multiple scenes in sequence and Tella stitches them together — great for structured courses or multi-part demos
  • Teleprompter: Built-in scrolling teleprompter so you can deliver to camera confidently
  • Backgrounds: Virtual backgrounds with blur, color, or custom images

Tella Limitations

  • Requires a good camera setup to get value from camera-forward features
  • More expensive than Loom for similar async use: Pro is $19/month
  • Less mature than Loom for team collaboration features
  • Not suitable for live streaming

Best For

Newsletters, course creators, coaches, and content marketers who want screen recordings that feel like premium video content rather than screen captures.

Feature Comparison

  • Free plan: OBS (unlimited) > ScreenPal (15 min) > Tella (limited) > Loom (25 videos) > Camtasia (30-day trial)
  • Ease of setup: Loom > ScreenPal > Tella > Camtasia > OBS
  • Video editing: Camtasia > Tella > ScreenPal > Loom (basic) > OBS (none)
  • Team collaboration: Loom > Tella > ScreenPal > Camtasia > OBS
  • AI features: Loom > Tella > ScreenPal > Camtasia > OBS
  • Price (paid): ScreenPal ($4/mo) > OBS (free) > Tella ($19/mo) > Loom ($15/mo) > Camtasia ($180/yr)

Our Recommendations by Use Case

For async team communication: Loom is the clear winner. The combination of instant sharing, AI summaries, and viewer engagement features is purpose-built for this use case.

For live streaming or complex setups: OBS Studio. No other tool comes close for free, and the plugin ecosystem handles almost any advanced requirement.

For educators and trainers on a budget: ScreenPal at $4/month gives you recording, editing, captions, and hosting without breaking the budget.

For professional training video production: Camtasia. If your videos are a core product or revenue driver, the investment in Camtasia's editing capabilities pays off.

For camera-forward content creation: Tella. If you're building a newsletter, course, or YouTube presence where your personality matters, Tella's design approach is worth the premium.

Related Reading

  • Best Project Management Tools for Startups in 2026
  • Best All-in-One Business Platforms for Solopreneurs in 2026
  • Notion vs Monday.com: Which Is Better in 2026?

title: Best Affiliate Marketing Tools in 2026
description: Running an affiliate program without the right software is a nightmare. We tested Rewardful, PartnerStack, FirstPromoter, Impact, and Systeme.io to find the best affiliate marketing tools for every budget.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Affiliate Marketing Software: Why It Matters More Than Ever

Word-of-mouth has always been the most effective marketing channel. Affiliate programs formalize that dynamic — you pay people a commission when they send you paying customers. Done right, it's one of the best-performing acquisition channels for SaaS, e-commerce, and digital products: low upfront cost, performance-based, and scalable without proportionally increasing headcount.

But running an affiliate program without dedicated software is painful. Tracking affiliate clicks and conversions manually, generating commission reports in spreadsheets, and processing payouts one by one will consume your team's time and introduce errors. The right affiliate marketing platform automates all of that.

We tested five leading affiliate tools by setting up real programs, recruiting test affiliates, running conversions through, and processing payouts. Here's an honest breakdown of each.

1. Rewardful — Best for SaaS on Stripe

Rewardful is purpose-built for SaaS companies using Stripe or Paddle as their payment processor. The setup takes under 30 minutes, and the Stripe integration is the tightest we've seen — affiliate commissions are automatically calculated from real Stripe charges, including recurring subscriptions, upgrades, and refunds.

What Makes Rewardful Stand Out

  • Stripe-native integration: Rewardful reads your actual Stripe data rather than relying on pixel tracking. This means commissions are accurate even when users don't complete a tracked session — they just need to use the coupon code or referral link.
  • Simplicity: The dashboard is clean and focused. Affiliates get a simple portal showing their links, conversions, and earnings. No unnecessary complexity.
  • Coupon code tracking: Affiliates can be assigned custom discount codes in addition to tracking links, which works better for podcast sponsors and influencers who can't always use links.
  • Reasonable pricing: Starter plan is $49/month for up to $7,500 in monthly affiliate revenue tracked. Plans scale with your affiliate revenue volume rather than flat user fees.

Rewardful Limitations

  • Only works with Stripe and Paddle — not an option if you use another payment processor
  • No marketplace or affiliate recruitment features — you bring your own affiliates
  • Limited advanced reporting compared to enterprise tools like Impact
  • Not ideal for non-subscription or physical product businesses

Best For

SaaS founders and digital product companies who process payments through Stripe and want a clean, reliable affiliate program without enterprise complexity or pricing.

2. PartnerStack — Best for B2B SaaS Partner Programs

PartnerStack goes beyond basic affiliate programs into full partner relationship management. It handles affiliates, resellers, referral partners, and agency partners — with different commission structures and dashboards for each partner type. If your go-to-market strategy includes a partner ecosystem, PartnerStack is the most complete platform for managing it.

What Makes PartnerStack Stand Out

  • Partner marketplace: PartnerStack has a marketplace of 80,000+ active partners who can discover and apply to your program. This is a significant advantage over tools that require you to recruit all your own affiliates.
  • Multi-tier partner management: Handle different partner types (affiliates, resellers, agencies) with separate portals, commission structures, and reporting — all in one platform.
  • Automated payouts: PartnerStack processes partner payments automatically in 200+ countries via PayPal, Stripe, or direct deposit. No manual payment processing.
  • B2B-optimized tracking: Longer attribution windows and multi-touch tracking designed for B2B sales cycles that can span weeks or months.

PartnerStack Limitations

  • Pricing is enterprise-oriented — starts around $500/month and goes up quickly. Not a fit for early-stage startups.
  • Overkill if you only need a basic affiliate program
  • More complex to set up and administer than simpler tools

Best For

Growth-stage B2B SaaS companies with dedicated partner programs that include multiple partner types and need a mature platform for recruiting, managing, and paying partners at scale.

3. FirstPromoter — Best All-Around Affiliate Platform

FirstPromoter sits in the middle of the market — more capable than Rewardful but without PartnerStack's enterprise scope and price. It works with any payment processor (Stripe, Chargebee, Braintree, Recurly, PayPal, and more), handles complex commission structures, and includes a solid affiliate portal that requires no technical configuration.

What Makes FirstPromoter Stand Out

  • Payment processor flexibility: FirstPromoter integrates with more payment processors than any other tool we tested, making it accessible to businesses not on Stripe.
  • Commission flexibility: Percentage commissions, flat-fee commissions, recurring commissions on subscriptions, one-time commissions on initial sales, and custom rules per product — all supported.
  • Affiliate portal: The built-in affiliate portal is white-labeled, easy to customize with your branding, and gives affiliates a clean dashboard for their links, stats, and earnings.
  • Two-tier affiliates: FirstPromoter supports two-tier affiliate structures (affiliates who recruit other affiliates) — useful for building larger referral networks.
  • Pricing: Starts at $49/month for the Starter plan — competitive with Rewardful for broader payment processor support.

FirstPromoter Limitations

  • No native affiliate marketplace — you source your own partners
  • Reporting is solid but not as advanced as Impact's analytics
  • Interface feels slightly dated compared to Rewardful

Best For

SaaS companies, digital product creators, and e-commerce businesses that need reliable affiliate tracking across multiple payment processors with flexible commission structures.

4. Impact — Best for Enterprise and E-Commerce at Scale

Impact (formerly Impact Radius) is the enterprise affiliate and partnership management platform. Major brands — including Shopify, Levi's, and Uber — use Impact to manage complex partner programs with hundreds or thousands of affiliates, influencers, and media partners. The platform's capabilities are genuinely impressive, but so are the cost and complexity.

What Makes Impact Stand Out

  • Advanced attribution: Impact's attribution modeling handles multi-touch journeys across affiliates, influencers, paid ads, and organic channels — giving you an accurate picture of what's actually driving conversions.
  • Contract automation: Dynamic commissioning rules, automated performance bonuses, and digital contract management are built in. High-performing affiliates can be automatically upgraded to better commission tiers.
  • Fraud detection: Impact includes real-time fraud detection and blocking — important when your program grows large enough to attract bad actors.
  • Reporting depth: Cohort analysis, LTV-based commissions, media cost reporting — Impact's analytics are the most sophisticated on this list.
  • Publisher marketplace: Access to a large marketplace of publishers, influencers, and media partners actively looking for programs to join.

Impact Limitations

  • Pricing is custom and typically starts at $500-1,000+/month — not accessible for most small businesses
  • Complex to set up and requires significant onboarding time
  • Overkill for programs with fewer than 50-100 active affiliates

Best For

Enterprise e-commerce brands, large SaaS companies, and retailers who need sophisticated partnership management, fraud prevention, and deep analytics across hundreds of partners.

5. Systeme.io — Best Built-In Affiliate Program for Digital Product Creators

Systeme.io takes a different approach to affiliate marketing: rather than being a standalone affiliate tool, it includes a complete affiliate program management system built into its all-in-one business platform. If you're selling digital products, courses, or running funnels on Systeme.io, you can launch an affiliate program without any additional software or integration.

What Makes Systeme.io Stand Out

  • Built into the platform: No integration required. Your affiliate program tracks sales from your Systeme.io funnels and courses automatically.
  • Affiliate marketplace: Systeme.io has its own marketplace where affiliates can discover your products. This provides organic affiliate recruitment without running your own outreach.
  • Price: Affiliate program features are included in all paid Systeme.io plans starting at $27/month. You're getting email marketing, funnels, course hosting, AND affiliate management — dramatically cheaper than buying each separately.
  • Easy affiliate portal: Affiliates get a simple dashboard showing their tracking links, conversions, and commissions. No technical setup required on either side.

Systeme.io Limitations

  • Only works for products sold through Systeme.io — not an external affiliate tracking tool
  • Commission reporting and analytics are basic compared to dedicated affiliate platforms
  • Not suitable for SaaS companies processing payments through Stripe or other external processors

Best For

Course creators, coaches, digital product sellers, and online entrepreneurs who want to add an affiliate program without adding another software subscription. If you're already on or considering Systeme.io for your funnels and email marketing, the built-in affiliate system is a major bonus.

Try Systeme.io free to see the affiliate management system alongside the full platform — the free plan lets you test the experience before committing to a paid plan.

Affiliate Tool Comparison at a Glance

  • Easiest setup: Systeme.io > Rewardful > FirstPromoter > PartnerStack > Impact
  • Payment processor support: FirstPromoter > Impact > PartnerStack > Systeme.io > Rewardful (Stripe/Paddle only)
  • Affiliate marketplace: PartnerStack > Impact > Systeme.io > Rewardful (none) > FirstPromoter (none)
  • Reporting depth: Impact > PartnerStack > FirstPromoter > Rewardful > Systeme.io
  • Price (entry): Systeme.io ($27/mo all-in) > Rewardful ($49/mo) ≈ FirstPromoter ($49/mo) > PartnerStack ($500+/mo) > Impact (custom)

How to Choose

Start with your payment processor and business model. If you use Stripe and run a SaaS, Rewardful is the fastest path to a working affiliate program. If you need payment processor flexibility, choose FirstPromoter. If you're running a B2B SaaS partner ecosystem, PartnerStack is worth the investment. For digital products on an all-in-one platform, Systeme.io eliminates the need for a separate affiliate tool entirely. And if you're managing a large-scale program with hundreds of partners, Impact is the professional choice.

Whatever tool you choose, launch the program before it's perfect. The affiliates you recruit in month one will teach you more about what commission structures and assets actually work than any amount of pre-launch planning.

title: Best Digital Signature Software in 2026
description: We sent real contracts through DocuSign, PandaDoc, SignNow, HelloSign, and Dropbox Sign to find the best digital signature software in 2026. Here's an honest breakdown of features, pricing, and real-world experience.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Digital Signatures Are Now a Legal and Business Standard

The days of printing a contract, signing it, scanning it, and emailing it back are effectively over for businesses that want to operate efficiently. Digital signatures are legally binding in the US (under ESIGN and UETA), the EU (eIDAS), and most major jurisdictions worldwide. More importantly, they eliminate the friction that delays deals and frustrates clients.

But choosing between the major e-signature platforms isn't straightforward. Pricing varies wildly. Feature sets differ significantly. And the "simple" tools often become inadequate as your contract volume grows. We tested five leading platforms by sending real contracts, negotiating terms with test recipients, and tracking the full signing experience end to end.

Quick Comparison

  • DocuSign: Most trusted brand, enterprise-grade, expensive at scale
  • PandaDoc: Best for sales teams — combine proposals, contracts, and signatures
  • SignNow: Best value for high-volume document signing
  • HelloSign (Dropbox Sign): Cleanest API, best for developers integrating e-signatures
  • Dropbox Sign: Same product as HelloSign (rebranded), good for Dropbox-heavy teams

1. DocuSign — The Industry Standard

DocuSign is the name most people think of when they hear "e-signature." It has the largest market share, the most enterprise integrations, and the brand recognition that sometimes matters in enterprise sales cycles (some clients specifically request DocuSign in contracts). For businesses that need an e-signature tool their counterparts will recognize and trust, DocuSign is the default choice.

What DocuSign Does Best

  • Trust and recognition: DocuSign's brand is synonymous with e-signatures. Sending via DocuSign signals professionalism to clients who might be skeptical of lesser-known platforms.
  • Compliance: DocuSign maintains SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, FedRAMP, HIPAA BAA, and 21 CFR Part 11 compliance certifications — more than any competitor. Essential for regulated industries.
  • Integrations: 400+ native integrations including Salesforce, Microsoft 365, Google Workspace, HubSpot, and most major CRMs. If your business runs on enterprise software, DocuSign likely connects to it.
  • Advanced authentication: Knowledge-based authentication (KBA), phone authentication, ID verification, and in-person signing support for high-stakes documents.
  • Global reach: 44 languages, local compliance in 180+ countries, and global customer support.

DocuSign Limitations

  • Price: DocuSign is the most expensive option. The Personal plan is $15/month for 5 envelopes — barely enough for a solo freelancer. Standard is $45/user/month. Business Pro is $65/user/month. At scale, you're looking at serious spend.
  • Template management can be clunky for teams with large document libraries
  • The interface feels dated compared to newer competitors
  • Customer support is inconsistent for non-enterprise plans

Best For

Enterprise companies, regulated industries (healthcare, finance, legal, government), and any business where brand recognition of the e-signature tool matters to clients or compliance requires deep certification.

2. PandaDoc — Best for Sales Teams

PandaDoc takes a fundamentally different approach: it's not just an e-signature tool but a complete document automation platform for sales teams. You build proposals, quotes, and contracts in PandaDoc, add products from a catalog, apply pricing rules, and collect signatures — all in one workflow. For B2B sales teams, this collapses three or four tools into one.

What PandaDoc Does Best

  • Document creation: PandaDoc's editor is the most capable on this list for creating beautiful, customized documents. Drag-and-drop layout, embedded videos, image galleries, and product tables.
  • Content library: Reusable content blocks, approved messaging, and pricing tables that sales reps insert into documents without starting from scratch or going off-script.
  • Analytics: Document analytics show you when the client opened the document, how long they spent on each section, and whether they forwarded it to others — intelligence that helps your sales team follow up at the right moment.
  • Workflow automation: Auto-send for approval, conditional content (different terms for different deal sizes), and CRM sync that updates deal status when documents are signed.
  • Pricing: Free plan includes unlimited documents and signatures with 5 documents/month. Essentials is $35/user/month.

PandaDoc Limitations

  • Template creation has a learning curve — the editor is powerful but not as simple as DocuSign's
  • Less suitable for legal or HR use cases that don't need the proposal-building features
  • Custom branding requires a higher-tier plan

Best For

B2B sales teams that want to create proposals and contracts in the same tool they use to collect signatures. If your sales cycle involves sending custom-quoted proposals, PandaDoc eliminates the need for a separate proposal tool.

3. SignNow — Best Value for Volume Signing

SignNow by airSlate is the best-kept secret in e-signature software. It offers more features at lower prices than DocuSign and competes seriously with HelloSign for API users. If you're signing a high volume of routine documents and don't need enterprise compliance certifications, SignNow delivers excellent value.

What SignNow Does Best

  • Price: Business plan is $20/user/month (billed annually) — less than half of DocuSign Standard and significantly below PandaDoc. For teams signing 50+ documents per month, the savings compound quickly.
  • Bulk sending: Send one document to hundreds of signers simultaneously with a single action — useful for HR onboarding, event waivers, and mass agreement updates.
  • Roles and workflows: Specify signing order, conditional routing (if Field A is checked, send to Person B), and parallel signing for multiple parties.
  • Kiosk mode: Allow in-person signing from a shared device — useful for events, stores, and intake forms.
  • Integrations: Native integrations with Salesforce, HubSpot, Microsoft Teams, Google Drive, and 30+ others. API available on Business Cloud and above.

SignNow Limitations

  • Brand recognition is lower than DocuSign — some enterprise clients may push back
  • Document editor is basic compared to PandaDoc
  • Compliance certifications are solid but not as extensive as DocuSign's list
  • Mobile app is functional but not the best in class

Best For

Small businesses, HR teams, and operations-focused companies that sign a high volume of routine documents and want to minimize per-seat software costs without sacrificing core functionality.

4. HelloSign / Dropbox Sign — Best for Developers and API Integration

HelloSign was rebranded as Dropbox Sign after Dropbox acquired it in 2019. The product is the same, and it remains one of the cleanest, most developer-friendly e-signature platforms available. The API is well-documented, the signing experience is the simplest and least intimidating of any platform we tested, and the Dropbox integration is seamless for teams already in that ecosystem.

What Dropbox Sign Does Best

  • Signing experience: The recipient experience is the smoothest on this list. Clean, minimal interface that doesn't confuse signers who aren't tech-savvy.
  • API quality: Dropbox Sign's API is well-documented with SDKs in multiple languages, sandbox testing, and detailed webhook support. Developers consistently rate it as the easiest e-signature API to integrate.
  • Dropbox integration: If your team stores files in Dropbox, the native integration is genuinely seamless — sign directly from Dropbox, automatic file storage back to Dropbox after signing.
  • Templates: Template creation is straightforward, and template links (shareable URLs that create a new document for each signer) are useful for high-volume recurring documents like NDAs and client agreements.
  • Pricing: Essentials plan is $20/month for 1 user and 5 documents/month. Standard is $30/user/month for unlimited documents.

Dropbox Sign Limitations

  • 5 document limit on the entry plan is restrictive for active users
  • Less capable than PandaDoc for document creation and proposal work
  • No built-in payment collection (unlike some competitors)
  • Enterprise compliance certifications are narrower than DocuSign's

Best For

Developers integrating e-signatures into applications, Dropbox-centric teams, and small businesses that prioritize a clean signer experience over advanced document creation features.

What About HelloSign vs Dropbox Sign?

HelloSign and Dropbox Sign are the same product. Dropbox rebranded HelloSign in 2022. If you see references to either name, they refer to identical software. All plans, features, and pricing are unified under the Dropbox Sign brand.

Feature Comparison

  • Brand trust: DocuSign > PandaDoc > Dropbox Sign > SignNow
  • Document creation: PandaDoc > DocuSign > SignNow > Dropbox Sign
  • API quality: Dropbox Sign > SignNow > DocuSign > PandaDoc
  • Value for money: SignNow > Dropbox Sign > PandaDoc > DocuSign
  • Compliance certifications: DocuSign > PandaDoc > SignNow > Dropbox Sign
  • Signer experience: Dropbox Sign > PandaDoc > SignNow > DocuSign

Legal Validity: What You Need to Know

All five tools create legally binding electronic signatures for standard business contracts under US law (ESIGN Act, UETA) and most international equivalents. The audit trail each tool generates — timestamp, IP address, email verification, signing certificate — is sufficient evidence of intent to sign for the vast majority of contract disputes.

Exceptions: some jurisdictions and document types require qualified electronic signatures (QES) with identity verification, or wet ink signatures (notarized documents, some real estate transactions, wills in certain states). If you're unsure about your specific document type, consult a lawyer — don't rely on the software vendor's marketing materials alone.

Our Recommendation

For most small businesses, SignNow or Dropbox Sign hits the right balance of features, usability, and price. If your sales workflow involves building custom proposals, PandaDoc is worth the premium. If you're selling to enterprise clients who expect DocuSign or you operate in a regulated industry, DocuSign is the safe choice despite its higher cost.

Whatever tool you choose, the most important thing is getting documents signed faster. Even the cheapest e-signature tool accelerates deal cycles compared to paper — and faster closed deals compound significantly over a year.

  • Best Free CRM Tools in 2026
  • Best All-in-One Business Platforms for Solopreneurs in 2026

title: Best Virtual Phone System for Small Business in 2026
description: We set up real business phone numbers and tested calls, texts, and team features across OpenPhone, Grasshopper, Google Voice, RingCentral, and Dialpad to find the best virtual phone system for small businesses in 2026.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Why Every Small Business Needs a Virtual Phone System

Using your personal cell number for business calls is a liability. Your clients have your personal number. You can't separate work hours from personal time. If you hire someone, they can't share your number. And if you ever want to sell the business or hand off client relationships, there's no professional phone presence to transfer.

A virtual phone system gives your business a real phone number (or multiple numbers), routes calls intelligently, enables texting from that number, and works from any device — your existing smartphone, laptop, or desk phone. Most plans cost less than $25/month per user, and setup takes under an hour.

We tested five leading virtual phone systems by setting up real business lines, making and receiving calls from multiple devices, testing SMS workflows, and evaluating team collaboration features. Here's what we found.

Quick Picks by Use Case

  • OpenPhone: Best for small teams and startups — modern, affordable, excellent UX
  • Grasshopper: Best for solopreneurs — simple, no per-user pricing, add-on to existing phone
  • Google Voice: Best free option — basic but reliable for solo operators
  • RingCentral: Best for growing businesses — full UCaaS platform with advanced call management
  • Dialpad: Best AI features — real-time transcription and AI coaching built in

1. OpenPhone — Best for Small Teams and Startups

OpenPhone is the virtual phone system built by former startup founders for startup teams. The design is clean, the mobile app is excellent, and the team collaboration features are genuinely useful in ways that older systems aren't. If you're a team of 2-20 people that needs a professional phone presence without enterprise complexity, OpenPhone is our top pick.

What Makes OpenPhone Stand Out

  • Shared numbers: Your whole team can share a single business number. Incoming calls and texts appear in a shared inbox. Any team member can respond. Call assignments and notes are visible to everyone — no more missed follow-ups because a call went to someone's personal device.
  • Modern app: OpenPhone's iOS and Android apps are among the best-designed in the category. Clean interface, fast switching between conversations, and reliable notifications.
  • Business texting: Full SMS and MMS from your business number, including scheduled messages, auto-replies, snippets (saved message templates), and contact notes. Texting is a first-class feature, not an afterthought.
  • Integrations: Native integrations with HubSpot, Salesforce, Slack, and Zapier. Call and text activity syncs to your CRM automatically.
  • Voicemail transcription: All voicemails are transcribed automatically and searchable in the app.
  • Pricing: Starter plan is $19/user/month. Business plan (adds IVR, call recording, analytics) is $33/user/month. Both include unlimited US and Canadian calls and texts.

OpenPhone Limitations

  • International calling requires add-on credits — not included in base plans
  • No desk phone support — cloud-only (desktop and mobile apps)
  • Video calling is not included — you'd need a separate tool like Zoom
  • Less feature-rich than RingCentral or Dialpad for large call center use cases

Best For

Startups, remote teams, small businesses with 2-20 employees who want a modern, collaborative phone system with excellent SMS capabilities and CRM integration.

2. Grasshopper — Best for Solopreneurs and Freelancers

Grasshopper takes a fundamentally different approach from other virtual phone systems: rather than replacing your existing phone number, it adds a business number on top of it. Your business calls forward to your personal cell. You answer with the Grasshopper app and can see it's a business call before picking up.

This makes Grasshopper ideal for solopreneurs and very small businesses that want a professional phone presence without setting up a separate communication system for the team.

What Makes Grasshopper Stand Out

  • Per-account pricing (not per-user): Unlike most virtual phone systems, Grasshopper charges per account rather than per user seat. The Solo plan is $31/month and includes 1 number and 3 extensions. The Partner plan is $51/month for 3 numbers and 6 extensions. If you have 2-3 people sharing a small number of lines, this pricing model is significantly cheaper than per-seat alternatives.
  • Simplicity: Grasshopper is designed to be simple. You set up your number, record a professional greeting, configure call forwarding, and you're done. No complex routing rules, no admin dashboards to learn.
  • No new device required: Business calls ring your existing phone through the Grasshopper app. You don't need a separate device or SIM card.
  • Vanity numbers: Grasshopper has an excellent selection of toll-free vanity numbers (1-800-YOUR-BIZ style) if that's important for your brand.

Grasshopper Limitations

  • Calls forward to your personal number — you don't get a separate business calling experience on your device
  • No shared team inbox for calls and texts
  • Limited integration with CRMs and business tools
  • SMS is basic compared to OpenPhone's business texting features
  • Not scalable for teams larger than 5-6 people

Best For

Freelancers, consultants, solopreneurs, and very small businesses (1-3 people) that need a professional phone number and basic call forwarding without paying per-user fees or learning a new phone system.

3. Google Voice — Best Free Option for Solo Operators

Google Voice for personal use is free and gives you a second phone number that forwards to your existing device. For the Google Workspace business version, pricing starts at $10/user/month and adds team management features. For solo operators who are extremely budget-conscious, Google Voice is a legitimate option — but it comes with meaningful limitations.

What Google Voice Does Best

  • Price: Free personal plan for US users. Business plans start at $10/user/month as part of Google Workspace.
  • Google integration: Seamless integration with Gmail, Google Calendar, and Google Meet — useful if your business already runs on Google Workspace.
  • Reliability: Google's infrastructure is rock-solid. Call quality and uptime are excellent.
  • Voicemail transcription: Accurate voicemail transcription delivered to your Gmail inbox.

Google Voice Limitations

  • No auto-attendant or IVR on the free plan
  • Business texting is limited — no SMS automation, templates, or shared team inbox
  • International calls require credits (not included)
  • Porting your Google Voice number to another carrier can be difficult
  • Business plan requires a Google Workspace subscription ($6-18/user/month) in addition to Voice
  • No CRM integrations on basic plans

Best For

Truly solo operators who need a free business number for basic inbound/outbound calls and are already deeply embedded in Google Workspace.

4. RingCentral — Best for Growing Businesses with Complex Needs

RingCentral is the comprehensive UCaaS (Unified Communications as a Service) platform — phone, video, team messaging, and contact center all in one. It's designed for businesses that are outgrowing simple virtual phone systems and need enterprise features: call queues, IVR trees, advanced analytics, call recording, and multi-location management.

What Makes RingCentral Stand Out

  • Feature completeness: RingCentral includes everything: auto-attendant, call queues, ring groups, IVR menus, call recording, live transcription, video meetings (up to 200 participants), team messaging, fax, and SMS — all in one platform.
  • Scalability: RingCentral scales from 2-person startups to 50,000+ employee enterprises. You won't outgrow it.
  • Integrations: 300+ native integrations including Salesforce, Microsoft Teams, Slack, Zendesk, and HubSpot.
  • International: Local numbers and calling plans in 100+ countries — important for businesses with international clients or remote teams.
  • Reliability: 99.999% uptime SLA backed by a financially credible company.

RingCentral Limitations

  • Price: Core plan starts at $30/user/month; Advanced is $35/user/month; Ultra is $45/user/month. More expensive than OpenPhone for comparable features at small team sizes.
  • Interface is complex — significantly more setup and learning curve than simpler tools
  • Overkill for teams under 10 people that don't need enterprise call management
  • Customer support quality has been inconsistent based on reviews

Best For

Companies of 20+ employees, businesses with call centers or high inbound call volume, and organizations that need advanced call routing, international capabilities, and enterprise integrations.

5. Dialpad — Best AI-Powered Features

Dialpad's defining feature is Vi, its built-in AI assistant that transcribes calls in real time, surfaces relevant information during conversations, detects customer sentiment, flags action items automatically, and provides post-call coaching. If you're in sales or customer support and want AI to make every call better, Dialpad is the most advanced option on this list.

What Makes Dialpad Stand Out

  • Real-time AI transcription: Every call is transcribed live. You can read back what was said without taking notes. Transcripts are searchable after the call.
  • AI call summaries: Dialpad automatically generates a call summary with key topics discussed, action items identified, and next steps — without you lifting a finger.
  • Sentiment analysis: Dialpad's AI monitors customer sentiment during the call and alerts managers when a call is going poorly in real time.
  • Sales coaching: For sales teams, Dialpad can flag when competitors are mentioned, when pricing objections come up, or when customers use specific keywords — and surface relevant coaching notes.
  • Pricing: Standard plan is $27/user/month. Pro is $35/user/month. Enterprise is custom.

Dialpad Limitations

  • AI features are impressive but require training and configuration to get maximum value
  • More expensive than OpenPhone or Google Voice for teams that don't use the AI features
  • Call quality on international routes can be inconsistent
  • SMS features are solid but not as polished as OpenPhone's

Best For

Sales teams and customer support operations that want AI-powered call intelligence, coaching, and automation. If your team is on calls for hours each day, Dialpad's AI features pay for themselves in time saved and performance improvement.

Feature Comparison

  • Price (entry, per user/mo): Google Voice (free/personal) > Grasshopper ($31/account) > OpenPhone ($19) > Dialpad ($27) > RingCentral ($30)
  • Ease of setup: Google Voice > Grasshopper > OpenPhone > Dialpad > RingCentral
  • Team collaboration: RingCentral > OpenPhone > Dialpad > Grasshopper > Google Voice
  • Business SMS: OpenPhone > Dialpad > RingCentral > Grasshopper > Google Voice
  • AI features: Dialpad > RingCentral > OpenPhone > Grasshopper > Google Voice
  • Scalability: RingCentral > Dialpad > OpenPhone > Grasshopper > Google Voice
  • CRM integrations: RingCentral > Dialpad > OpenPhone > Grasshopper > Google Voice

What to Look for When Choosing

Before selecting a virtual phone system, answer these questions:

  • How many users need the system? Per-user pricing (OpenPhone, Dialpad, RingCentral) makes sense for larger teams; per-account pricing (Grasshopper) can be cheaper for 1-3 people sharing lines.
  • How important is SMS? If texting clients is core to your workflow, OpenPhone is the clear winner. If you rarely text, this matters less.
  • Do you need a physical desk phone? RingCentral and Dialpad support traditional desk phones; OpenPhone and Grasshopper are cloud-only.
  • Do you have complex call routing needs? IVR menus, call queues, and ring groups are available in RingCentral and Dialpad from the start; OpenPhone includes basic versions; Grasshopper and Google Voice are limited here.
  • What CRMs do you use? Check native integration support before committing — sync with your CRM saves significant manual work.

Our Recommendation

For most small businesses, OpenPhone offers the best combination of modern features, excellent mobile app, team collaboration, and affordable per-user pricing. It's where we'd start for any team of 2-15 people.

If you're truly solo and budget-conscious, Google Voice (personal) or Grasshopper work well without per-user costs. For businesses with a focus on sales coaching and AI-powered call intelligence, Dialpad justifies its price. And for large or complex organizations that need enterprise call management, RingCentral is the comprehensive choice.

Most platforms offer free trials. Set up your top two options, make some calls, and see which one your team actually uses naturally — adoption matters more than feature lists.

  • Best All-in-One Business Platforms for Solopreneurs in 2026
  • Best Online Payment Tools for Small Business in 2026

title: Best Project Portfolio Management Tools 2026
description: Compare the top project portfolio management tools for 2026. Monday.com, Asana, Wrike, ClickUp, and Smartsheet reviewed for teams managing multiple projects at once.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Managing Multiple Projects Is a Different Challenge

Running a single project is hard enough. Running a portfolio of five, ten, or twenty concurrent projects — each with its own team, timeline, budget, and stakeholders — is an entirely different discipline. Project portfolio management (PPM) tools exist to give leadership teams a birds-eye view across all active work, so nothing falls through the cracks and resources aren't overcommitted.

In 2026, the best PPM tools go far beyond basic Gantt charts. They offer workload balancing, cross-project dependency tracking, portfolio-level reporting, and real-time resource allocation. We tested five of the most popular options to help you find the right fit.

Quick Comparison: Top PPM Tools at a Glance

  • Monday.com: Best all-around portfolio management with flexible views
  • Asana: Best for organizations running structured, process-driven portfolios
  • Wrike: Best for enterprise teams needing advanced reporting and approvals
  • ClickUp: Best value for growing teams wanting maximum customization
  • Smartsheet: Best for spreadsheet-native teams transitioning to PPM

1. Monday.com — Best Overall Portfolio Management

Monday.com has evolved from a simple task board into a full work operating system. Its portfolio management features allow teams to create a master dashboard that aggregates status, progress, and timeline data from every individual project board.

Standout Portfolio Features

  • Portfolio dashboards: Build executive-level views that pull live data from all connected project boards. See RAG status, completion percentages, and budget rollups in one place.
  • Workload view: Visualize how much each team member is assigned across all projects simultaneously, preventing burnout and bottlenecks.
  • Automations: Trigger status updates, notifications, and escalations automatically when milestones are missed or risks emerge.
  • Custom views: Switch between Kanban, Gantt, calendar, and timeline views depending on the stakeholder audience.

What Could Be Better

Monday.com's pricing scales by seat, which makes it expensive for large organizations. Advanced portfolio reporting requires the Enterprise plan. Some teams find the flexibility overwhelming without strong internal governance.

Pricing

Basic starts at $9/seat/month. Pro (recommended for PPM) runs $16/seat/month. Enterprise pricing is custom.

2. Asana — Best for Process-Driven Portfolios

Asana's Portfolio feature (available on Business and Enterprise plans) is purpose-built for leadership teams that need to monitor multiple workstreams without diving into individual task details. Every project added to a portfolio automatically reports its status, timeline health, and goal alignment.

Standout Features

  • Goals integration: Connect portfolio projects directly to company-level OKRs. Progress rolls up automatically as work completes.
  • Portfolio status reports: Generate shareable status snapshots with one click, pulling live data from all projects in the portfolio.
  • Workload management: See team capacity across projects and reassign work with drag-and-drop simplicity.
  • Rules and automations: Automate repetitive handoffs, approvals, and status changes so PMs spend less time on admin.

What Could Be Better

Asana's time tracking is limited without third-party integrations. The interface can feel rigid compared to more flexible tools like ClickUp. Reporting customization is more limited than Wrike.

Pricing

Portfolios require the Business plan at $24.99/user/month (billed annually).

3. Wrike — Best for Enterprise Reporting and Approvals

Wrike is the go-to PPM platform for larger organizations with complex approval workflows, compliance requirements, and advanced reporting needs. Its cross-project analytics and custom dashboards give operations leaders the visibility they need to manage a diverse project portfolio.

Standout Features

  • Advanced analytics: Build custom reports across the entire portfolio, filtering by team, status, budget, or any custom field.
  • Approval workflows: Route deliverables through structured review and sign-off chains before projects advance.
  • Resource management: Plan and rebalance resources across projects with capacity planning tools built into the portfolio view.
  • Blueprints: Create reusable project templates so new projects launch with consistent structure every time.

Pricing

Business plan starts at $24.80/user/month. Enterprise and Pinnacle tiers are available for large organizations with advanced needs.

4. ClickUp — Best Value for Growing Teams

ClickUp delivers an exceptional amount of functionality for its price, making it the top choice for growing teams that want enterprise-grade PPM features without enterprise pricing. Its Portfolios feature aggregates project data, and its highly customizable views let teams work the way they think.

Standout Features

  • Everything view: See all tasks, across all projects, filtered and sorted however makes sense for your role.
  • Goals and OKRs: Link projects to team goals and track progress automatically.
  • Custom fields: Track budget, risk, RAG status, or any other metric at the portfolio level.
  • Free plan: Generous free tier makes it easy for small teams to start before committing to paid plans.

Pricing

Free plan available. Unlimited at $7/user/month. Business at $12/user/month covers most PPM use cases.

5. Smartsheet — Best for Spreadsheet-Native Teams

Smartsheet bridges the gap between the spreadsheet workflows most business teams already know and the structured portfolio management capabilities they need to scale. If your team lives in Excel and Google Sheets today, Smartsheet is the most natural upgrade path.

Standout Features

  • Familiar grid interface: Works like a spreadsheet but with formulas, automation, and real-time collaboration built in.
  • Portfolio rollup sheets: Automatically aggregate data from individual project sheets into a master portfolio view.
  • Dashboards: Build visual portfolio dashboards with charts, KPI widgets, and status indicators that update in real time.
  • Resource management add-on: Available as an add-on for capacity planning across the portfolio.

Pricing

Pro at $9/user/month. Business at $32/user/month (includes advanced portfolio features). Enterprise pricing is custom.

How to Choose the Right PPM Tool

The right tool depends on your organization's size, complexity, and how your teams already work. Here's a quick decision guide:

  • Small to mid-size teams: Start with ClickUp or Monday.com for the best combination of flexibility and value.
  • Process-driven organizations: Asana's structured approach and OKR integration make it a natural fit.
  • Large enterprises with compliance needs: Wrike's advanced reporting and approval workflows are worth the premium.
  • Spreadsheet-heavy teams: Smartsheet minimizes the learning curve while adding real PPM power.

If your team also needs a complete business operating system that covers CRM, email marketing, and project funnels in one platform, Systeme.io is worth exploring as a complementary hub for client-facing workflows alongside your PPM tool.

  • Best Time Tracking Software 2026
  • Best Website Analytics Tools 2026

title: Best Customer Feedback Tools 2026
description: Find the best customer feedback tools for 2026. We compare Hotjar, Canny, UserVoice, Typeform, and Productboard to help you capture, organize, and act on user insights.
author: TopStackTools Team
content:
Why Customer Feedback Tools Are Non-Negotiable in 2026

The companies that grow consistently in 2026 aren't guessing what customers want — they're listening systematically. Customer feedback tools give product, marketing, and customer success teams a structured way to capture user sentiment, identify friction points, prioritize features, and close the loop with customers who raised issues.

But not all feedback tools serve the same purpose. Some excel at behavioral analytics (watching what users actually do), others at structured feature request management, and others at high-volume survey collection. We tested five of the most widely used options across real SaaS products and service businesses.

Quick Comparison: Which Tool Fits Your Use Case

  • Hotjar: Best for understanding user behavior through heatmaps and session recordings
  • Canny: Best for managing feature requests and customer-driven product roadmaps
  • UserVoice: Best for enterprise teams with structured feedback and product planning workflows
  • Typeform: Best for beautiful, high-completion-rate surveys and form-based feedback
  • Productboard: Best for product teams that need to connect customer feedback directly to roadmap decisions

1. Hotjar — Best for Behavioral User Research

Hotjar is the industry standard for understanding how users actually interact with your product or website — not just what they say about it. Through heatmaps, session recordings, and on-site surveys, Hotjar reveals the gap between stated preferences and real behavior.

Core Features

  • Heatmaps: Click, move, and scroll heatmaps show exactly where users focus attention and where they give up.
  • Session recordings: Watch individual user sessions to identify confusion, rage clicks, and drop-off moments.
  • Feedback widgets: Embed one-click sentiment widgets on any page so users can flag issues in the moment they experience them.
  • Surveys: Trigger on-site surveys based on user behavior — exit intent, time on page, or specific URL visits.

What Could Be Better

Hotjar is primarily a research and observation tool, not a structured feedback management system. It doesn't organize feedback into themes or feed into a product roadmap. You'll need a complementary tool for that.

Pricing

Free plan available with limited recordings. Plus starts at $32/month. Business from $80/month with advanced filters and integrations.

2. Canny — Best for Feature Request Management

Canny solves a specific but critical problem: what do you do with the endless stream of feature requests that come in through support tickets, sales calls, and social media? Canny creates a structured, public-facing board where customers can submit and upvote requests, giving your product team a prioritized signal of what matters most.

Core Features

  • Public feedback boards: Customers submit ideas and vote on existing ones. The upvote count becomes a real prioritization signal.
  • Changelog: When you ship a feature, announce it to the customers who requested it. They get notified automatically.
  • Roadmap view: Share a public or private roadmap that shows the status of requested features.
  • Integrations: Connect to Jira, Linear, GitHub, Slack, and Intercom so feedback flows directly into your development workflow.

Pricing

Free for up to 100 tracked users. Growth plan at $79/month. Business plan at $359/month for larger teams.

3. UserVoice — Best for Enterprise Feedback Programs

UserVoice has been the enterprise feedback standard for over a decade. Its platform is designed for organizations where feedback comes from multiple channels — sales, support, surveys, and direct user submissions — and needs to be aggregated, analyzed, and connected to strategic product decisions.

Core Features

  • Multi-source feedback capture: Collect feedback from support tickets, NPS surveys, sales calls, and direct submissions in one place.
  • Idea prioritization: Score and rank feature ideas by revenue impact, customer segment, and strategic alignment.
  • Executive reporting: Generate stakeholder-ready reports showing which customer segments are asking for what.
  • Smart inbox: Automatically categorize incoming feedback by theme using AI-assisted tagging.

Pricing

UserVoice is enterprise-priced. Contact their team for current pricing — typically starts at several hundred dollars per month for full platform access.

4. Typeform — Best for High-Quality Survey Feedback

Typeform's conversational survey format consistently achieves completion rates 2-3x higher than traditional form tools. If your primary feedback mechanism is surveys — NPS, CSAT, onboarding feedback, or exit surveys — Typeform's design makes the experience enjoyable enough that users actually finish.

Core Features

  • Conversational format: One question at a time keeps users engaged and reduces abandonment.
  • Logic jumps: Show different questions based on previous answers for highly personalized surveys.
  • Integration ecosystem: Connect responses directly to HubSpot, Salesforce, Slack, Notion, and 500+ other tools.
  • Beautiful templates: Hundreds of pre-built survey templates for NPS, product feedback, event registrations, and more.

If you're collecting leads through feedback surveys, consider pairing Typeform with Systeme.io — its built-in email automation lets you follow up with respondents automatically based on their answers, creating a seamless lead nurture flow without extra tools.

Pricing

Basic plan free with limited responses. Plus at $25/month. Business at $83/month. Enterprise pricing available.

5. Productboard — Best for Connecting Feedback to Roadmap

Productboard is purpose-built for product managers who need to go from raw customer feedback to prioritized roadmap decisions. It sits between your feedback collection tools and your development workflow, acting as the intelligent layer that helps you understand what to build next and why.

Core Features

  • Insights repository: Import feedback from Intercom, Zendesk, Salesforce, and other sources. Tag and link insights to specific features.
  • Feature prioritization: Score features using a customizable priority matrix that weights customer impact, effort, and strategic value.
  • Roadmap builder: Create multiple roadmap views for different audiences — engineering, executive, customer-facing.
  • Portal: Share a public feedback portal where customers can submit and vote on ideas.

Pricing

Starter at $19/maker/month. Pro at $59/maker/month. Scale pricing is custom for larger organizations.

Building a Complete Feedback System

The most effective feedback programs don't rely on a single tool. A common setup: Hotjar for behavioral research, Typeform for structured surveys, and Canny or Productboard for managing what to do with the insights. The key is closing the loop — when customers see that their feedback shaped a shipped feature, they become active advocates.

Pair your feedback stack with a solid internal knowledge base in project management tools so feedback themes are visible across the whole team, not just the product manager's inbox.
